page: Amazon Web Services m8a.xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an AMD EPYC 9R45 processor with 4 dedicated physical cores and 4 vCPUs operating at a clock speed of 4.5 GHz. The system is equipped with 16.0 GB of DDR5 memory running at 6 GHz, providing 4.0 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it offers a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates top-tier single-core performance. The instance achieves top 10% placement in stress-ng single-core CPU tests, Geekbench single-core scores, and single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression. Text processing and text generation for small LLM models also rank in the top tier. Conversely, multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and multi-threaded decompression fall into the average category. Database operations, Redis SET operations, and static web serving throughput also demonstrate average performance characteristics.

From a resource density perspective, the m8a.xlarge provides a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. The dedicated CPU allocation and DDR5 memory generation offer modern hardware standards, but the lack of local storage requires external network-attached storage, which may impact overall I/O latency. The 2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth represents a potential bottleneck for data-intensive workloads. The instance delivers high single-core efficiency but average multi-core scaling relative to its hardware profile.

This server is configured for general-purpose workloads that benefit from high single-core CPU speeds and modern instruction sets like AVX-512. Suitable applications include web serving, text processing, software compilation, and lightweight database operations. It is also capable of handling small-scale LLM text generation tasks.
description: Amazon Web Services m8a.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 4 dedicated AMD EPYC vCPUs running at 4.5 GHz and 16.0 GB of DDR5 memory. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, it lacks local storage and dedicated GPUs, offering a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU performance, single-threaded compression, and small-model LLM text generation, while multi-core operations, memory bandwidth, and database performance remain average. The 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio offers balanced resource density, though the lack of local storage and modest network baseline are notable tradeoffs. This instance is suited for general-purpose workloads, software compilation, text processing, and web serving.
